title: Untitled
research_problem: The paper investigates pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward different pedestrian facilities (signalized/unsignalized intersection crosswalks, midblock crosswalks, physical barriers, shelters, colored paving) in a divided urban boulevard next to Michigan State University.
method_summary: Mixed-methods study combining: (1) video-camera observation of pedestrian movements using 8 cameras at study site over multiple sessions during peak periods, and (2) user survey administered electronically to 711 pedestrians (daily and occasional users) covering crossing patterns, preferences, and perceptions.
datasets: Grand River Avenue (M-43), East Lansing MI - 1km divided boulevard section from Abbott to Bogue Streets
Survey data: 711 completed questionnaires (from 897 received, after removing 186 non-user or incomplete responses)
Video data: 16-18 hours of recorded pedestrian movements across study site

main_claims.claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian facility (83% reported preference) with high crossing compliance rate (71.2%)
Crosswalk location relative to pedestrian origin/destination is the most influential decision factor (90% said so)
Pedestrian signal presence significantly influences crossing decisions at specific locations (74% reported influence)
Physical barriers (vegetation and concrete walls) influence pedestrian crossing decisions significantly (65% reported influence)
Good agreement exists between self-reported survey behavior and actual observed pedestrian behavior

limitations.text: Study conducted at single location (university campus corridor) - limited geographic generalizability
Survey response rate was 17.9% - potential for non-response bias
Sample predominantly university affiliates (students, staff, faculty) - may not represent general population
Data collection limited to Michigan State University session periods (Spring/Fall 1998), excluding weekends and adverse weather
Self-reported survey data may be subject to social desirability bias

notes: Study site was renovated Grand River Avenue corridor with multiple facility types in same environment. SCCR averages 71.4% across all crosswalk types. Signalized crosswalks showed highest SCCR (82.8%) but lower temporal compliance. Survey responses showed good consistency with observed behavior (59% spatial compliance from survey vs 71.4% observed).
